author: Sofia Martínez
title: Local Keyword Research in the AI Era: Tools, Tactics, and Real Data
description: How to find local search opportunities when Google AI Overviews and answer engines are reshaping what rank, and which tools actually work in 2026.

sections.p: Local keyword research changed in 2025. Google AI Overviews now appear on 64% of local searches—'best coffee near me', 'plumber open now', 'pediatrician accepting new patients'—pulling answers directly from Google Business Profiles and featured snippets instead of traditional blue links. This means traditional keyword volume metrics are less reliable. You can't just look at search volume and assume clicks are there. We've had to rebuild how we research keywords for local clients, and the new approach is actually simpler and more profitable.

sections.h2: The Local Keyword Research Landscape Has Shifted

sections.p: Here's what we're seeing: high-intent local keywords still drive clicks, but they're now competing with AI-generated answers. A search for 'emergency dentist [city]' might show Google's AI overview listing 3-4 options pulled from Google Business Profiles, then traditional organic results below. The winner isn't necessarily whoever ranks #1 organically—it's whoever has the best Google Business Profile (shows in the AI overview) AND ranks in top 3 organically. This changes our keyword strategy entirely. We no longer chase every possible keyword variation. We focus on keywords that convert and that we can actually own (Google Business Profile + content + backlinks).

One pest control company we worked with was targeting 'pest control near me', 'exterminator [city]', 'termite treatment [city]'. These keywords had 2,400, 880, and 320 monthly searches respectively in their metro. But Google AI Overviews were dominating. We shifted focus to 'termite inspection cost [city]', 'rodent control cost [city]', 'bed bug treatment [city]'—lower volume (120-240 monthly), but no AI overview dominating. They ranked #1 for two of these within 8 weeks, and these keywords converted at 18% (someone calling to get a quote or book an inspection) versus 6% for the high-volume keywords buried under AI answers.

sections.h2: Tools That Actually Work for Local Keyword Research in 2026

list: Google Search Console (free, most reliable for your actual local search traffic; see what queries already drive clicks)
Google Keyword Planner (free Google Ads tool; local search volume by city/zip, updated monthly)
SE Ranking or Semrush (local keyword difficulty, SERP feature data, AI overview detection)
Answer Engine Optimization (AEO) tools like Perplexity Analytics and Bing Copilot Studio (see what AI engines are saying about your keywords)
AnswerThePublic (shows actual search variations people type; more useful than 'related searches')
Your Google Business Profile Insights (underrated; shows actual search terms driving visits and calls)

sections.p: Most local businesses ignore their Google Business Profile Insights. This is a mistake. You can literally see 50+ search queries that people used to find you, how many clicks each got, how many calls it generated. One plumbing company we audited discovered that 'frozen pipe thaw [city]' was their 5th most-clicked search (14 clicks per month), but they'd never published content about it. We created one 400-word post answering 'How to Thaw a Frozen Pipe Safely' and optimized their Google Business Profile description for that term. Within 6 weeks, that keyword was generating 34 clicks monthly (143% increase), and their call volume went up 12% that quarter.

sections.h2: From Keywords to Content: The Local Research-to-Content Workflow

sections.p: Here's how we operationalize local keyword research in 2026: (1) Pull your top 30 search queries from Google Business Profile Insights. (2) For each, check Google Search for AI Overviews (if present, note it). (3) Check local search volume and difficulty in Semrush or SE Ranking. (4) Identify 8-12 keywords with moderate difficulty (30-50 difficulty score), monthly search volume above 40, and no dominant AI Overview. (5) Create one pillar piece of content per keyword. This isn't random blogging—it's keyword-to-content mapping.

A physical therapy clinic used this workflow. They identified that 'physical therapy for rotator cuff [city]' had 65 monthly searches, difficulty of 38, and no AI Overview dominating. They created a 1,200-word post on rotator cuff physical therapy, optimized for that keyword, added structured data, and linked it from their Google Business Profile. Within 5 weeks, they ranked #2 for that keyword (23 clicks/month), and the conversion rate was 22% (someone requesting an appointment). The post became their highest-ROI piece of content.

sections.h2: Keyword Research Adjustments for Answer Engines

sections.p: Perplexity, Grok, and Copilot are eating search volume. These answer engines cite sources but don't drive clicks the way traditional search does. When we research keywords, we now check: 'Does this keyword's answer come from an AI overview or answer engine?' If yes, we look for question-based variations that are less likely to trigger AI answers. 'Rotator cuff injury' might have a dominant AI overview. 'How long does rotator cuff physical therapy take?' is a more specific question that AI engines are less likely to summarize perfectly—it needs expert context from your content.

One law firm targeting 'DUI defense' noticed their traffic was declining (AI Overviews handling general 'what is a DUI' searches). We restructured their keyword strategy to target 'DUI defense cost [state]', 'DUI first offense penalty [state]', 'how to beat a DUI charge [state]'—more specific, less AI-friendly. Their organic traffic increased 28% in 3 months because they were targeting questions that required nuanced, expert answers AI couldn't handle from a snippet.

Here's where small local businesses have a massive advantage over national brands: you can actually test keywords. A national brand can't do local keyword testing—it's too complex. But you? Create one blog post for a keyword you're unsure about. Track results for 6 weeks. If it converts, expand. If it doesn't, move on. This gives you permission-based research advantage. You're not guessing at keyword value—you're measuring it.

sections.quote: In the AI era, the most valuable local keywords aren't the high-volume ones. They're the specific, intent-heavy questions that AI can't answer in a single paragraph. Those are the keywords that drive calls.
